summary_text:
S:
The patient reports knee pain, primarily in both knees, exacerbated by activity, particularly cycling over the past few days. He describes stiffness in the mornings and soreness by the end of the day, most noticeable around dinner time. He denies any other systemic symptoms such as fever, fatigue, or abdominal pain. He reports no injury and attributes the pain to increased cycling mileage. He is currently taking aspirin daily and Crestor, reporting no problems with either medication. He denies any chest pain or shortness of breath.

O:
The patient's vital signs are stable. Physical examination reveals bilateral mild edema and erythema of the knees, with a small effusion noted on the right knee. Palpation of the knees elicits tenderness. Range of motion is decreased bilaterally. An EKG demonstrates normal sinus rhythm. Right knee X-ray is unremarkable, showing no fracture or bony abnormalities. Dragon shows an II/VI systolic ejection murmur, which is stable from previous examinations.

A:
The patient's primary concern is bilateral knee pain, likely related to a possible flare of lupus given his history. The pain is not consistent with acute injury or other common causes. The patient's coronary artery disease is stable.

P:
Order an autoimmune panel to evaluate for lupus activity. Order a lipid panel and an echocardiogram to assess cardiovascular risk. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess response to treatment and review lab results. Counsel the patient on the importance of consistent aspirin use and adherence to his medication regimen. Dragon, finalize the note.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Joint pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. James Moore is a 57-year-old male with a past medical history significant for coronary artery disease and lupus, who presents today with some joint pain.

The patient reports he has been " putting a few more miles on the bike each day ". His bilateral knees have been sore in the last 3 to 4 days. He states his bilateral knees are equally painful. He notes stiffness and soreness in the morning and, by the end of the day, he feels the same sensation. He endorses swelling and redness in his knees which reduces in severity by the afternoon. The patient denies any other associated fatigue. He denies any injury to his knees. He denies pain in any other joints including his elbows, wrists, or shoulders.

In regards to his coronary artery disease, Mr. Moore states he is compliant with his aspirin and Crestor. He denies chest pain or shortness of breath.

The patient denies recent symptoms related to his lupus over the last year.

He denies fever, chills, abdominal pain, nausea, and vomitting.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Constitutional: Denies fevers.
• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain or dyspnea.
• Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath.
• Musculoskeletal: Endorses joint pain, stiffness, swelling, and erythema in bilateral knees.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, stable.
• Musculoskeletal: Edema and slight erythema of bilateral knees. Slight effusion of the right knee with pain to palpation. Decreased range of motion in bilateral knees.

RESULTS

X-ray of the right knee is unremarkable. No bony abnormalities or fractures were noted.

Electrocardiogram stable in comparison to last year.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. James Moore is a 57-year-old male with a past medical history significant for coronary artery disease and lupus, who presents today with some joint pain.

Lupus.
• Medical Reasoning: This has been stable and asymptomatic for the past year, but the patient reports increased joint pain and inflammation in his bilateral knees lately. ​His recent knee x-ray was unremarkable, so I am concerned that this is a lupus flare.
• Additional Testing: We will order an autoimmune panel for further evaluation.

Coronary artery disease.
• Medical Reasoning: He is asymptomatic at this time. He has discontinued Plavix but continues with daily aspirin and Crestor. His recent EKG was unremarkable.
• Additional Testing: We will order a lipid panel and a repeat echocardiogram.
• Medical Treatment: Continue current regimen.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: Dragon shows an II/VI systolic ejection murmur, which is stable from previous examinations.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The clinical content (a stable systolic ejection murmur as an exam finding) is correctly in the Objective section, but the phrasing 'Dragon shows' reflects dictation software rather than an observation. This is an organizational/wording artifact rather than a true section error.

This is a dictation command, not a clinical plan item. It does not belong in any SOAP clinical section; if present at all, it should be outside the SOAP structure. Its presence in the Plan could momentarily confuse a reader about whether it is an action item.
